text.title: Mobile UX Principles
text.summary: Mobile-first thinking, mobile context factors, five key design principles, and iOS vs Android platform differences.

rule.statement: Design for the smallest screen first, identify core content and features, then add enhancements for larger screens.
rule.rationale: Designing for mobile first forces focus on essential content. Scaling down a desktop design produces a cramped, unusable result because mobile has fundamentally different input, attention, and network constraints.

list.item: Attention: partial attention, frequent interruptions — never rely on sustained focus.
Time: short sessions, quick tasks — design for task completion in under 60 seconds for core flows.
Location: variable environments (sun glare, one-handed use, movement).
Connection: slow or unreliable network — 53% of users abandon if load exceeds 3 seconds.
Input: touch, sometimes voice — no hover, no right-click, no precision pointer.
Screen: small and varying sizes with high pixel density.

rule.statement: Provide exactly one primary action per screen.
rule.rationale: Multiple competing actions force users to make decisions under the cognitive constraints of mobile context (partial attention, glare, movement). One primary action removes this decision.

rule.statement: Place primary actions in the thumb zone (lower third of screen).
rule.rationale: Reachability for one-handed use is physical. Primary actions placed in the hard-to-reach upper zone increase task abandonment on phones held in the dominant hand.

list.item: Focus: one primary action per screen.
Reachability: important actions in the thumb zone (lower third).
Context: leverage device capabilities (GPS, camera, biometrics).

rule.statement: Follow platform conventions for navigation, modals, and primary actions — do not port iOS patterns to Android or vice versa.
rule.rationale: Users have strong mental models from their platform. Cross-platform pattern violations produce "feels wrong" friction that is impossible to attribute in usability tests but measurable in task success rates.

list.item: Navigation: iOS uses bottom tab + back gesture; Android uses bottom nav + system back button.
Touch targets: iOS minimum 44x44pt; Android minimum 48x48dp; spacing 8px between targets.
Modals: iOS uses bottom sheets; Android uses full-screen overlays or dialogs.
Primary actions: iOS right side of screen; Android floating action button.
